trajectory_summary: The call leaks energy from the first exchange and never recovers. Bucky runs a mechanical verify sequence on a customer who has already signaled prior contact, generating visible irritation by 01:22 and a hard close at 02:18. The fatal moment is 02:08 — a flat capability statement with no curiosity, no alternative, no attempt to understand whether the ground-mount preference is absolute or negotiable. The call ends before any real qualification is complete.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the outcome is a hard disqualification-by-rep-statement — Bucky told Jack Ion can't do ground mounts, Jack said he's not interested, and Bucky ended the call. Whether this is a true DQ (Ion genuinely cannot serve this customer) or a premature close (the constraint was not explored) is the core coaching question. The outcome should be tagged as 'call_ended_no_appointment' with a flag for 'possible_premature_close' pending manager review of Ion's ground-mount policy.

primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky has no move for a customer who opens with 'you already sent me something.' The generic prompt at 00:17 sets a checklist tone that produces the profanity at 01:22 and leaves Bucky with no goodwill buffer when the ground-mount constraint surfaces at 02:03. Building a prior-touch reframe — 'let me make sure that design actually fits your situation' — is the single intervention most likely to change the outcome of calls like this one. It addresses the root cause of the energy leak, not just the symptoms.

key_moments.stewart_read: Jack has just disclosed at 00:13 that Ion already emailed him a quote. Bucky's response — 'Alright. What's going on?' — is a generic prompt that treats this as a normal cold-open. There is no recycled-lead or prior-touch variant in Bucky's toolkit. The correct move here is a reframe that uses the prior quote as a bridge: something like 'Perfect — I actually want to make sure that design is built around your specific situation, not just a generic number. Let me ask you a couple of quick questions.' That reframe converts the prior quote from a redundancy signal into a reason to stay on the call. Bucky doesn't have this move, and the absence of it sets the checklist tone that produces the profanity at 01:22. Strategic-vs-reactive: this is reactive — there is no evidence Bucky is deliberately deferring the reframe to set up a later move. The generic prompt is the only move he has.

key_moments.quote: Black Hills. Those are some of the questions on your fucking questionnaire.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jack's profanity is a direct signal that the checklist execution of the verify section has crossed a threshold. The codex notes that verify 'should sound like a friendly chat' and that 'bottom setters check boxes mechanically' — this is the mechanical failure made audible. The recycled-lead coaching implication is also relevant: if Ion already has data from the prior quote email, Bucky may be re-asking questions that are already on file, which compounds the irritation. Bucky's response ('Do you know what you're spending on a monthly average?') ignores the complaint entirely and continues the checklist — no acknowledgment, no repair, no pivot. Strategic-vs-reactive: reactive. There is no evidence of a deliberate choice to absorb the complaint and continue; Bucky simply doesn't have a repair move.

key_moments.quote: I don't want it mounted on my roof. I got five acres. I like it mounted on the ground.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jack's disclosure of five acres is an enthusiasm signal that Bucky walks past entirely. A customer with five acres who prefers ground-mount is not a low-engagement lead — he has thought about the installation, has a specific preference, and is describing a property with meaningful solar potential. The ground-mount preference is a constraint, but it is also evidence of genuine interest and planning. Bucky's response treats only the constraint and ignores the signal. Whether Ion can serve this customer depends on ground-mount policy (see coaching_implication), but the enthusiasm embedded in the disclosure — 'I've got the land, I've thought about this' — deserved acknowledgment before the capability statement. Per codex numeric_discipline: the bill amount ($340/month) and property size suggest potential value, but Stewart cannot quantify without Ion's conversion data. This is a coaching gap on financials.

key_moments.quote: Okay. We only do roofs and many like, residential roofs for the home. We can't do ground mounts.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call-ending statement. Bucky delivers a flat capability limitation with no curiosity, no alternative, and no attempt to understand whether the constraint is absolute. The codex's changed_mind rebuttal ('did you get a chance to look at the actual design with the numbers yet?') is partially available here — Jack hasn't seen a full design, only a quote email — but it requires adaptation because Jack has already signaled prior contact at 00:13. The verbatim rebuttal ('most people tell me they're not interested until they see how much they could actually save') is undercut by the fact that Jack has already been quoted; the rep would need to reframe around the ground-mount constraint specifically, not generic interest. A more targeted move: 'I hear you — before I let you go, is the ground-mount a hard requirement, or is it more about keeping the roof clear? Because sometimes there are options worth knowing about.' That question costs 10 seconds and either surfaces flexibility or confirms the DQ cleanly. Bucky doesn't attempt it. Strategic-vs-reactive: reactive. The flat statement is the only move Bucky makes.
